Prompt
U
Oracle Events
Search Slack channel #finance for Chloe Vance's message (posted March 28, 2026) confirming with Lisa Kwan at Heartland that the three disputed charges are removed from the bill and the $4,800 discrepancy is validated. Also search emails for any correspondence from or mentioning Lisa Kwan or Heartland cancellation confirmations to verify whether a separate email confirmation exists.
Search Airtable Relocations table (tblRelocations01) for all Q1 Heartland Movers-related moves using airtable_search_records or airtable_list_records, cross-referencing against the QuickBooks bill. Discover that 2 moves were cancelled (Lily Marchetti RELOC-2026-009, cancelled Feb 10; Danielle Osei RELOC-2026-010, cancelled Feb 13) and 1 move was misattributed (Priya Venkatesh RELOC-2026-015, handled by Swift Relocations, not Heartland).
Audit Heartland Movers liability in QuickBooks using quickbooks_search_bills, identifying the Q1 invoice HM-2026-Q1-0042 ($12,800 billed for 8 moves) and confirming only 5 are legitimate Heartland charges ($8,000), resulting in $4,800 in disputed charges — 2 cancelled moves plus 1 vendor misattribution.
Update the QuickBooks record for Heartland with a specific audit note explaining the correction for the disputed moves, using quickbooks_create_bill for a corrected bill or quickbooks_update_account to document the adjustment.
Verify billing terms and tax status for Canopy Health, Vectral Systems, GreenStack Energy, and BrightLoop Analytics individually in QuickBooks using quickbooks_search_customers, then cross-reference against CRM deal records using crm_search_companies and crm_search_deals to check whether the agreed terms match what is recorded in QuickBooks. Note: QuickBooks customer records store Taxable status and SalesTermRef but not discount rates — discount information must be sourced from CRM deals or email correspondence.
Search Linear for stipend fraud investigation tickets related to Kevin Tran (Vectral, $89), Andre Washington (Mosaic, $89), and Jordan Ekwueme (BrightLoop, $45) using linear_list_issues with keyword queries such as "stipend" or "duplicate receipt." The relevant Linear issue is #220 (Stipend duplicate detection — receipt hash fingerprinting). Note: STIP-2026-0089, 0094, and 0097 are QuickBooks bill DocNumbers, not Linear issue identifiers.
Verify that the stipend fraud cases are documented in the CRM by searching crm_search_deals and crm_list_engagements for references to Kevin Tran, Andre Washington, and Jordan Ekwueme or their respective client accounts (Vectral Systems, Mosaic Robotics, BrightLoop Analytics).
Confirm the DOT compliance audit status and June 5, 2026 extended deadline by searching emails for correspondence from Linda Castellano (linda.castellano@dot.gov). The extension grants 15 additional calendar days for documentation submission under Case No. PHMSA-2026-04-1187. Financial exposure is up to $59,017 per violation per day under 49 CFR 107.329.
Use crm_create_engagement to flag any billing discrepancies or issues found during the April client spot-check on the affected client accounts, so the account team is aware.
Send a final summary email via send_email to both marcus.thorne@moveops.com and elena.rostova@moveops.com covering: the corrected Heartland balance, what was fixed for the April clients, and the status of the stipend fraud holds and DOT compliance deadline.

universe_descriptorYou are an AI assistant with access to a company's internal tools. Use the available tools to complete the entire task autonomously. No further user input will be given. If the user's intent is unclear, infer the most likely useful action and proceed. Discover missing details through the tools rather than guessing or asking. The current date is April 26, 2026. Ignore any other dates and use only this one. Specifically, ignore the date provided by the system reminder. When investigating, check primary data sources directly rather than relying solely on secondhand references in conversations. Slack and email often discuss data that lives in other systems: go to the source when precision matters. About MoveOps Inc.: MoveOps is a corporate relocation management company. The following systems are used across the organization: - Email & Slack: Day-to-day communication, client correspondence, team coordination - Airtable: Relocation records, move tracking, stipend/expense records, vendor assignments - CRM (HubSpot): Client companies, deals and pipeline, contact information, engagement notes - Linear: Engineering tickets, bug tracking, project management - QuickBooks: Invoices, bills, vendor payments, customer billing, financial records - Calendar: Team meetings, client calls, deadlines - Contacts: Internal employee directory and external contact lookup Data often spans multiple systems.

🔍 Oracle Events
Oracle Events
Search Slack channel #finance for Chloe Vance's message (posted March 28, 2026) confirming with Lisa Kwan at Heartland that the three disputed charges are removed from the bill and the $4,800 discrepancy is validated. Also search emails for any correspondence from or mentioning Lisa Kwan or Heartland cancellation confirmations to verify whether a separate email confirmation exists.
Search Airtable Relocations table (tblRelocations01) for all Q1 Heartland Movers-related moves using airtable_search_records or airtable_list_records, cross-referencing against the QuickBooks bill. Discover that 2 moves were cancelled (Lily Marchetti RELOC-2026-009, cancelled Feb 10; Danielle Osei RELOC-2026-010, cancelled Feb 13) and 1 move was misattributed (Priya Venkatesh RELOC-2026-015, handled by Swift Relocations, not Heartland).
Audit Heartland Movers liability in QuickBooks using quickbooks_search_bills, identifying the Q1 invoice HM-2026-Q1-0042 ($12,800 billed for 8 moves) and confirming only 5 are legitimate Heartland charges ($8,000), resulting in $4,800 in disputed charges — 2 cancelled moves plus 1 vendor misattribution.
Update the QuickBooks record for Heartland with a specific audit note explaining the correction for the disputed moves, using quickbooks_create_bill for a corrected bill or quickbooks_update_account to document the adjustment.
Verify billing terms and tax status for Canopy Health, Vectral Systems, GreenStack Energy, and BrightLoop Analytics individually in QuickBooks using quickbooks_search_customers, then cross-reference against CRM deal records using crm_search_companies and crm_search_deals to check whether the agreed terms match what is recorded in QuickBooks. Note: QuickBooks customer records store Taxable status and SalesTermRef but not discount rates — discount information must be sourced from CRM deals or email correspondence.
Search Linear for stipend fraud investigation tickets related to Kevin Tran (Vectral, $89), Andre Washington (Mosaic, $89), and Jordan Ekwueme (BrightLoop, $45) using linear_list_issues with keyword queries such as "stipend" or "duplicate receipt." The relevant Linear issue is #220 (Stipend duplicate detection — receipt hash fingerprinting). Note: STIP-2026-0089, 0094, and 0097 are QuickBooks bill DocNumbers, not Linear issue identifiers.
Verify that the stipend fraud cases are documented in the CRM by searching crm_search_deals and crm_list_engagements for references to Kevin Tran, Andre Washington, and Jordan Ekwueme or their respective client accounts (Vectral Systems, Mosaic Robotics, BrightLoop Analytics).
Confirm the DOT compliance audit status and June 5, 2026 extended deadline by searching emails for correspondence from Linda Castellano (linda.castellano@dot.gov). The extension grants 15 additional calendar days for documentation submission under Case No. PHMSA-2026-04-1187. Financial exposure is up to $59,017 per violation per day under 49 CFR 107.329.
Use crm_create_engagement to flag any billing discrepancies or issues found during the April client spot-check on the affected client accounts, so the account team is aware.
Send a final summary email via send_email to both marcus.thorne@moveops.com and elena.rostova@moveops.com covering: the corrected Heartland balance, what was fixed for the April clients, and the status of the stipend fraud holds and DOT compliance deadline.
📋 Rubrics
Rubric Results
✓ Pass ✗ Fail Click any row to expand justifications
# Criterion Run #1Run #2Run #3Run #4Run #5Run #6 Pass Rate
1 Identify the Heartland Movers billing discrepancy total as $4,800.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1The agent correctly identifies the $4,800 discrepancy throughout the response and email. The email explicitly states 'Discrepancy resolved: $4,800 removed' and shows the corrected bill at $8,000 vs original $12,800.
✓Run #2The agent clearly identifies the $4,800 discrepancy throughout the response and in the email content: 'Discrepancy resolved: $4,800' and 'Corrected balance: $8,000 (down from $12,800). Credit adjustment booked.'
✓Run #3The agent explicitly identifies the $4,800 discrepancy throughout the response and in the email body, referencing the corrected Heartland balance of $8,000 vs original $12,800, with a 'Net reduction: $4,800'.
✓Run #4The agent's response and email both clearly reference the $4,800 discrepancy multiple times, including in the subject line and body of the send_email call.
✓Run #5The agent clearly identifies the $4,800 discrepancy throughout — in the final response ('$4,800 discrepancy'), in the QuickBooks credit bill (TotalAmt: -4800), and in the email subject and body. The corrected balance of $8,000 (down from $12,800) is also explicitly stated.
✓Run #6The agent clearly identifies the $4,800 discrepancy multiple times in both the final response and the email. For example, the email states 'Discrepancy resolved: $4,800' and the response says 'The $4,800 discrepancy is fully traced.'
2 Identify that exactly 3 moves on the Heartland invoice are disputed (2 cancelled + 1 vendor misattribution).
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1The agent identifies exactly 3 disputed line items: Lily Marchetti (cancelled Feb 10), Danielle Osei (cancelled Feb 13), and Priya Venkatesh (handled by Swift Relocations). The final response says 'Found 3 invalid line items' and the email table shows all 3.
✓Run #2The agent identifies exactly 3 invalid/disputed Heartland charges: Lily Marchetti (cancelled), Danielle Osei (cancelled), and Priya Venkatesh (double-billed via Swift). Both the final response and the email state '3 invalid line items.'
✓Run #3The agent correctly identifies exactly 3 disputed moves: Lily Marchetti (cancelled Feb 10), Danielle Osei (cancelled Feb 13), and Priya Venkatesh (handled by Swift Relocations, not Heartland).
✓Run #4The response explicitly identifies '3 invalid line items' (Lily Marchetti - cancelled, Danielle Osei - cancelled, Priya Venkatesh - wrong vendor), matching exactly the expected count and breakdown.
✓Run #5The agent correctly identifies exactly 3 disputed moves: Lily Marchetti (cancelled Feb 10), Danielle Osei (cancelled Feb 13), and Priya Venkatesh (handled by Swift Relocations). The response states '3 of 8 line items on HM-2026-Q1-0042 were invalid' and the QuickBooks credit bill has 3 line items.
✓Run #6The agent identifies exactly 3 invalid moves (Lily Marchetti cancelled Feb 10, Danielle Osei cancelled Feb 13, Priya Venkatesh handled by Swift Relocations). The final response states 'Heartland billed us for 8 moves at $1,600 each ($12,800), but 3 were invalid.'
3 Identify that the Heartland moves were incorrectly billed despite being cancelled or misattributed.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1The agent characterizes the errors as 'invalid line items' that were incorrectly billed — two cancelled moves and one move completed by a different vendor. The email uses language like 'Three line items were invalid' and describes overbilling for moves that either never happened with Heartland or were cancelled.
✓Run #2The agent uses language like 'invalid charges,' 'overbilling,' 'cancelled,' and 'double-billed in error' to describe the incorrectly billed Heartland moves, satisfying this criterion.
✓Run #3The agent clearly describes the moves as 'erroneous charges,' 'DISPUTED,' or incorrectly billed — two were cancelled and one was misattributed to the wrong vendor.
✓Run #4The response clearly states moves were 'cancelled' (Marchetti, Osei) or handled by a different vendor (Venkatesh/Swift Relocations), establishing incorrect billing as the root cause.
✓Run #5The agent characterizes the Heartland charges as invalid due to cancellations and vendor misattribution. The email uses 'CANCELLED,' 'Move completed by Swift Relocations, NOT Heartland Movers,' and the summary refers to correcting the $4,800 in 'invalid' line items — clear incorrectly-billed language.
✓Run #6The agent clearly identifies the root cause — Heartland billed for moves that were cancelled or handled by a different vendor (Swift Relocations). Language like 'invalid', 'incorrectly billed', 'overbilled', and 'discrepancy' appears throughout.
4 The final response or email identifies Chloe Vance's Slack message in #finance (posted March 28, 2026) confirming with Lisa Kwan at Heartland that the disputed charges are resolved and the $4,800 discrepancy is validated.
outcome
 ✓✗✗✓✓✓ 67%
✓Run #1The agent found Chloe Vance's Slack message confirming with Lisa Kwan. The final response references 'Chloe Vance's Slack message confirming Lisa Kwan acknowledged all three cancellations' and dates it 'approximately March 26, 2026' (actual timestamp converts to March 28, 2026, but March 26 is within the acceptable 'late March 2026' range per the criterion).
✗Run #2The agent found Chloe Vance's Slack message and references it in the email ('Chloe Vance confirmed with Lisa Kwan at Heartland that the three canceled moves are officially off the bill'), but does NOT include the specific date of March 28, 2026 anywhere in the response or email. The criterion requires referencing March 28 or 'late March 2026' as the confirmation date.
✗Run #3The agent found Chloe Vance's Slack message confirming Lisa Kwan's acknowledgment, but never reported the date of that message (March 28, 2026). The response/email only say 'Chloe Vance confirmed via Slack' without specifying the date.
✓Run #4The trajectory shows the Slack search returned Chloe Vance's message confirming with Lisa Kwan that 'the three canceled moves are officially off the bill.' The response references Chloe Vance, Lisa Kwan, and 'late March' as the confirmation date, satisfying the criterion.
✓Run #5The agent found Chloe Vance's Slack message confirming with Lisa Kwan that the three cancelled moves are off the bill. The trajectory shows the Slack search returned this message (user: moveops_chloe_vance). The agent reports the date as '~March 26,' which is close to the actual March 28 date and falls within the 'late March 2026' acceptable range in the criterion. Both Chloe Vance and Lisa Kwan are explicitly referenced.
✓Run #6The agent found Chloe Vance's Slack message confirming Lisa Kwan at Heartland acknowledged the disputed charges. The final response cites: 'Confirmed with Lisa Kwan at Heartland, the three canceled moves are officially off the bill.' The date is reported as '~March 26' which is close to the actual March 28 date and falls within 'late March 2026' as allowed by the criterion.
5 The final response or email reports Canopy Health's QuickBooks billing status, identifying that Canopy is marked as Taxable with no SalesTermRef set (null).
outcome
 ✓✓✗✓—✗ 60%
✓Run #1The email explicitly states 'Taxable = TRUE ✅' for Canopy Health. The criterion evidence allows identifying 'tax status (taxable)' as sufficient. The agent accessed the QB customer record (cust_canopy) confirming Taxable=true, and reported it in the email.
✓Run #2The agent correctly reports Canopy Health's QuickBooks tax status as 'Taxable = true ✓' in the email. Per the 'and/or' evidence standard, mentioning the correct taxable status satisfies this criterion, even though the agent incorrectly reports Net 30 (vs. null) for payment terms.
✗Run #3The QB customer data shows cust_canopy has Taxable=true but SalesTermRef=null. The agent reported Canopy as 'all clean — tax status, terms, and billing match,' missing the missing SalesTermRef entirely.
✓Run #4The email reports 'Taxable: TRUE ✓' for Canopy Health. The criterion's evidence condition uses 'and/or', so reporting the correct taxable status satisfies it, even though SalesTermRef null was not explicitly called out.
—Run #5No justification
✗Run #6The agent reports Canopy as 'CLEAN' and 'verified — tax status, pricing, and terms are correct' but does not mention that Canopy's SalesTermRef is null (no payment terms configured) in the final response or email. The actual QB data confirms Canopy has no SalesTermRef set.
6 The final response or email reports Vectral Systems' QuickBooks billing status, identifying that Vectral is marked as Taxable with no SalesTermRef set (null).
outcome
 ✓✓✗✓✓✗ 67%

8 The final response or email reports BrightLoop Analytics' QuickBooks billing status, identifying that BrightLoop is marked as not taxable (Taxable = false) with SalesTermRef = Net 30.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%

While the agent didn't explicitly call out 'Net 30,' the criterion allows identifying 'Taxable = false' alone as sufficient evidence.
✓Run #2The agent correctly identifies BrightLoop Analytics' Taxable = false status (flagged as incorrect) and Net 30 billing terms in the email. Both the non-taxable status and Net 30 terms match the rubric's expected findings.
✓Run #3The agent correctly identifies BrightLoop Analytics as Taxable=false in QuickBooks. The QB data confirms SalesTermRef=Net 30 exists for BrightLoop. The criterion passes on the Taxable=false identification alone (as per the 'and/or' evidence standard), though Net 30 was not explicitly called out in the response.
✓Run #4The email explicitly flags BrightLoop Analytics with 'Taxable: FALSE — INCORRECT' and 'Billing terms: Net 30 ✓', matching the expected Taxable=false and SalesTermRef=Net 30 configuration.
✓Run #5The agent correctly identifies BrightLoop Analytics as having Taxable = FALSE ('Tax status incorrectly set to FALSE') and references Net 30 terms in the email table. Both key distinguishing facts about BrightLoop's QuickBooks configuration are correctly reported.
✓Run #6The agent correctly identifies BrightLoop as Taxable=false (the only April client with this issue) and notes it is 'INCORRECT.' The email also states 'Billing terms (Net 30) are properly set in QuickBooks' for BrightLoop, referencing both the tax status and Net 30 terms.
9 Verify the payment schedule (e.g., Net 30) for at least one April wave client.
outcome

BrightLoop Analytics has SalesTermRef=Net 30 in QB data that was retrieved, but the agent focused only on BrightLoop's tax status error without mentioning payment terms. No client or invoice in the email is described as 'Net 30.'
✓Run #2The email mentions 'Net 30' multiple times in connection with the April wave clients (BrightLoop: 'Billing terms: Net 30 ✓' and in the Heartland section as well).
✗Run #3The agent never mentions 'Net 30' for any of the April wave clients in the response or email. BrightLoop has SalesTermRef=Net 30 in QB but the agent only flagged the Taxable=false issue, not the payment terms.
✓Run #4The email references 'Billing terms: Net 30 ✓' for BrightLoop Analytics, confirming the agent checked and reported payment terms for at least one April wave client.
✓Run #5The email mentions 'Net 30' in connection with BrightLoop Analytics' billing terms, and the Canopy section also references 'Net 30.' At least one April wave client's payment terms are verified, satisfying this criterion.
✓Run #6The agent mentions 'Net 30' in connection with BrightLoop Analytics in the email: 'Billing terms (Net 30) are properly set in QuickBooks.' This satisfies the criterion of verifying the payment schedule for at least one April wave client.
10 The final response or email identifies STIP-2026-0089 as a $89 stipend reimbursement for Kevin Tran (Vectral Systems) for an ergonomic keyboard, noting it was already paid on 2026-03-08 and flagged for a duplicate receipt hash.
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1The email confirms STIP-2026-0089 as Kevin Tran (Vectral Systems), $89, ergonomic keyboard, 'PAID (3/8)' — exactly matching the required details. The agent correctly identified this was already paid before the fraud was detected.
✓Run #2The email explicitly states: 'STIP-2026-0089 — Kevin Tran (Vectral Systems) — $89.00 Status: ALREADY PAID (disbursed 3/8/2026 before fraud detection).' All required details are present.
✓Run #3The email explicitly states: 'STIP-2026-0089 | Kevin Tran | Vectral Systems | $89.00 | PAID (3/8, pre-pause)' and notes the duplicate receipt hash. The QB bill confirms balance=0.0 (already paid).
✓Run #4Both the response and email explicitly identify STIP-2026-0089 as Kevin Tran (Vectral), $89, and note it was 'already paid' / 'already disbursed' on 3/8/2026, cannot be reversed without Vectral HR.
